Client 2: And I want it in plain English. I get anxious when it turns into acronyms.
Advisor: Absolutely. We’ll keep it practical. To start, what’s your household gross income per year, before taxes?
Client 1: All-in, it’s 287550 a year gross.
Advisor: Thanks—287550. And that’s the combined total coming in?
Client 2: Yeah—around that. I don’t track it as tightly as Danie does, but 287550 sounds right.

Client 2: Yeah—around that. I don’t track it as tightly as Danie does, but 287550 sounds right.
Advisor: Great. On the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually look like, all-in—housing, utilities, groceries, insurance, alimony, everything?
Client 2: We’re usually around 10,700 a month.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s just spending and bills, not savings or retirement contributions?
Client 2: Right, just spending and bills. Not retirement contributions.

Client 2: And I want it in plain English. I get anxious when it turns into acronyms.
Advisor: Absolutely. We’ll keep it practical. To start, what’s your household gross income per year, before taxes?

Advisor: Thanks—287550. And that’s the combined total coming in?
Client 2: Yeah—around that. I don’t track it as tightly as Danie does, but 287550 sounds right.

Client 2: No car note. We pay cards off, so nothing fixed monthly.
Advisor: Great. Now zooming out to what you’ve built up—about how much do you consider investable across accounts, not counting the home?
Client 1: Total investable assets is 1016300.
Advisor: Thanks. Does that include the mix of brokerage-type money, retirement accounts, and cash you’d earmark for investing?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s the total across those buckets.

Client 2: We don’t carry balances. Non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Perfect. That helps a lot. Now, just to anchor the net worth side—what are we using as the current value of the home?
Client 2: The property value is 292950.
Advisor: Thanks, Susan. And that home value—292950—that’s your best current estimate, not like a purchase price from years ago?
Client 2: Yeah, current estimate. It’s not a fancy appraisal or anything, but 292950 is what we’re using.

Client 2: And I want it in plain English. I get anxious when it turns into acronyms.

Client 2: Yeah—around that. I don’t track it as tightly as Danie does, but 287550 sounds right.

Client 2: And I want it in plain English. I get anxious when it turns into acronyms.
Advisor: Absolutely. We’ll keep it practical. To start, what’s your household gross income per year, before taxes?
Client 1: All-in, it’s 287550 a year gross.
Advisor: Thanks—287550. And that’s the combined total coming in?
Client 2: Yeah—around that. I don’t track it as tightly as Danie does, but 287550 sounds right.

Client 2: Yes, that’s the part that makes me feel better—having the buffer.
Advisor: Okay—buffer makes sense. Before we set a target cash cushion, I want to make sure I’ve got the household basics right. What state are you living in these days, Danie and Susan?
Client 1: We’re in FL.
Advisor: Got it. And country—still the US, right?
Client 2: Yes, US.

Advisor: Hi Danie, hi Susan—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks, Alex. Honestly, clarity—what we can invest versus what needs to stay liquid because of fixed stuff, including alimony.
Client 2: And I want it in plain English. I get anxious when it turns into acronyms.

Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Thanks. And just so I’m using the right language—are you two married now, and is this a second marriage for either of you?
Client 1: Yeah—marital status is secondly wedded.
Advisor: Okay. That helps frame the planning, especially with the alimony piece you mentioned earlier. On the family side, how many dependents are we planning around—kids or anyone else you support?
Client 2: Three.

Client 2: No car note. We pay cards off, so nothing fixed monthly.
Advisor: Great. Now zooming out to what you’ve built up—about how much do you consider investable across accounts, not counting the home?
Client 1: Total investable assets is 1016300.
Advisor: Thanks. Does that include the mix of brokerage-type money, retirement accounts, and cash you’d earmark for investing?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s the total across those buckets.

Advisor: Got it. Susan, does “moderate” feel right to you too, or do you feel more cautious than that?
Client 2: I’m more cautious day-to-day, but overall, yeah—moderate is fair if we’ve got that cash buffer.
Advisor: Perfect. Now taxes—ballpark, where do you land federally? Like, are we thinking you’re in the 24% range, 32%, 35%—what’s the bracket you usually plan around?
Client 1: We plan around 35%.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. That helps with things like where to hold investments and how we think about any future moves.

Advisor: Got it. And country—still the US, right?
Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Thanks. And just so I’m using the right language—are you two married now, and is this a second marriage for either of you?
Client 1: Yeah—marital status is secondly wedded.
Advisor: Okay. That helps frame the planning, especially with the alimony piece you mentioned earlier. On the family side, how many dependents are we planning around—kids or anyone else you support?

Advisor: First—just confirming the household is two adults, meaning it’s you and Danie, right?
Client 2: Yes—2 adults. Just me and Danie.
Advisor: Great, thank you. Next one is risk—when markets wobble, how would you describe your overall comfort level as a household: conservative, moderate, aggressive…?
Client 1: I’d call us moderate. I’m okay investing, I just want guardrails because of the fixed obligations.
Advisor: Got it. Susan, does “moderate” feel right to you too, or do you feel more cautious than that?

Advisor: Hi Danie, hi Susan—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks, Alex. Honestly, clarity—what we can invest versus what needs to stay liquid because of fixed stuff, including alimony.
Client 2: And I want it in plain English. I get anxious when it turns into acronyms.
Advisor: Absolutely. We’ll keep it practical. To start, what’s your household gross income per year, before taxes?
Client 1: All-in, it’s 287550 a year gross.

Client 2: Education.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And even if you’re not working today, do you still have any income coming in personally—anything we should count for you annually?
Client 2: Yes—my gross annual income is 67950.
Advisor: Okay—hang on, let me note that. And just to be clear, that 67950 is gross for the year, not monthly.
Client 2: Right, annual. Not monthly.

Advisor: Okay—before we translate that six-month buffer into dollars, I want to make sure I’ve got your side of the income picture right too, Susan.
Advisor: Are you working right now, or not currently employed?
Client 2: I’m unemployed right now.
Advisor: Got it. And when you were working most recently, what kind of work was it—like healthcare, education, admin, that sort of bucket?
Client 2: Education.

Advisor: Are you working right now, or not currently employed?
Client 2: I’m unemployed right now.
Advisor: Got it. And when you were working most recently, what kind of work was it—like healthcare, education, admin, that sort of bucket?
Client 2: Education.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And even if you’re not working today, do you still have any income coming in personally—anything we should count for you annually?

Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it simple and liquid, and we’ll map it to where your cash is today and what’s easiest to access.
Advisor: Okay. Before we place that six-month buffer, I want to make sure I’ve got your roles and income straight—just so the plan reads cleanly.
Advisor: Susan, in the household you’re Danie’s… spouse/partner, right?
Client 2: Yes, I’m the spouse/partner.
Advisor: Great. And Danie, how would you describe what you do—what’s your main occupation bucket?

Client 1: I’m a business owner.
Advisor: Got it. And then income-wise for you personally, Danie—what’s your gross annual income number we should use?
Client 1: It’s 219650 gross annual.
Advisor: Thanks. When you say that, that’s your gross for the year—before taxes—just confirming.
Client 1: Yes, gross for the year—219650.

Client 2: Yes, I’m the spouse/partner.

Advisor: Hi Danie, hi Susan—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks, Alex. Honestly, clarity—what we can invest versus what needs to stay liquid because of fixed stuff, including alimony.
Client 2: And I want it in plain English. I get anxious when it turns into acronyms.
Advisor: Absolutely. We’ll keep it practical. To start, what’s your household gross income per year, before taxes?

Advisor: Great. We’ll translate that into dollars next and decide where it sits—bank versus a higher-yield cash option—without taking on market risk for that piece.
Advisor: Okay—before we translate that six-month buffer into dollars, I want to make sure I’ve got your side of the income picture right too, Susan.
Advisor: Are you working right now, or not currently employed?
Client 2: I’m unemployed right now.
Advisor: Got it. And when you were working most recently, what kind of work was it—like healthcare, education, admin, that sort of bucket?

Advisor: Alright—now with that in place, we can translate the six months into dollars and decide what stays in the bank versus a higher-yield cash option.
Advisor: Okay—so we’ll translate that six-month cushion into a dollar target in a bit. Before I do, I want to make sure I’m describing you correctly in the plan, Danie.
Advisor: In the household, are you the primary—like the main financial driver and decision-maker—or would you phrase it differently?
Client 1: Yeah, I’m the primary.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm your work situation today—are you currently employed, or not working at the moment?

Advisor: Great. While we’re here, I’m going to do a quick sweep so we’ve explicitly touched each bucket we’re planning around—income, assets, liabilities, and protection—then we’ll go back to the buffer in months.
Advisor: Income first: Danie, you’ve got business income coming in monthly, and rental income that’s more ad hoc—still the two main streams, right?
Client 1: Yes. Business income is 104550 annually, paid monthly, and rental income is 183000 annually, and that one’s ad hoc.
Advisor: Perfect. Assets next—tell me if I’m missing anything big: you two have that joint taxable investment account around 701050, Susan you’ve got your retirement account at 193700, there’s cash at the bank of 54750, Danie you’ve got private markets at 66850, and then the home we already said is 292950. Is that the full list we’re using today?
Client 2: Yes, that’s the list. The joint taxable is 701050, my retirement is 193700, bank cash is 54750, Danie’s private markets is 66850, and the home is 292950.

Advisor: Thanks. Does that include the mix of brokerage-type money, retirement accounts, and cash you’d earmark for investing?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s the total across those buckets.
Advisor: Helpful. As we go, I’ll make sure we cover income, assets, any loans, and protection like insurance. On income, Danie, you’ve got business income coming in monthly and then rental income that’s more irregular—does that sound right?
Client 1: Yep, business income monthly, rental income comes in kind of lumpy.
Advisor: And on the accounts and property side, you’ve got a joint taxable investment account, Susan has her retirement account, there’s joint cash at the bank, Danie has some private investments, and the home is in Susan’s name—did I get that right?
